critic_raw:
This unsupervised fMRI clustering vs. cognitive test Alzheimer's classification design has a fundamental methodological contradiction at its core and several evaluation problems.

The primary pipeline uses unsupervised clustering (k-means) to assign cluster labels, then trains a supervised classifier on those cluster labels to predict clinical stage. This is a two-stage pipeline where the first stage (clustering) is unsupervised and the second stage (classification) is supervised. The k selection (k=3, 4, or 5) uses silhouette score on the validation set — but silhouette score is an unsupervised quality measure of cluster cohesion, not a measure of how well clusters align with clinical labels. Selecting k by silhouette score on the validation set provides no guarantee that the selected k produces clusters that discriminate between CN/MCI/ADD. A k that produces compact, well-separated clusters may still not align with clinical stages if the neuroimaging data clusters by scanner site, head motion, or other nuisance factors rather than disease status. The correct selection criterion for k would be the supervised classification performance on the validation set, not the unsupervised silhouette score.

The functional connectivity matrix computation and PCA/ICA dimensionality reduction are performed using statistics from the entire dataset before splitting. For fMRI data, this means the mean correlation structure and principal components are estimated from the test participants' brain data. In a dataset with ~1,000-1,500 participants, computing whole-brain correlation matrices for 400 ROIs across all participants and then splitting means the connectivity matrices for test participants were normalized and decomposed using information from the test set. This is a particularly acute leakage concern because functional connectivity patterns systematically differ between CN/MCI/ADD — the group-level connectivity statistics in the full-dataset PCA encode disease status information.

The study has n ≈ 1,000-1,500 unique participants but n ≈ 5,000 scans (multiple scans per participant over 5 years). The split uses stratified random sampling of scans, which means the same participant's scans can appear in both training and test sets. The design acknowledges this briefly ('the model must generalize to unseen individuals, not simply memorize patterns from repeated scans') but the split procedure described (random sampling of observations) does not enforce participant-level exclusivity. This is a critical flaw: a model that sees participant X's scan from year 1 in training can memorize participant X's connectivity signature and correctly classify their year 3 scan in the test set based on participant identity rather than disease status.

The statistical test for significance is specified as 'paired-sample t-tests or McNemar test' on the test set. These tests are appropriate for different scenarios: McNemar for binary outcomes comparing two classifiers on the same test set; paired t-test for continuous differences. For a three-class classification task evaluated by weighted macro F1, neither is the natural choice — a permutation test or DeLong's test on AUC would be more appropriate. Selecting the wrong test can produce anticonservative p-values.

The primary model is described as 'Logistic regression or XGBoost' — selecting between these two model classes is itself a hyperparameter search that should be specified as occurring on the validation set, not left ambiguous. If both are evaluated and the better one is selected, this constitutes an additional degrees-of-freedom in model selection.
all_issues_raised: k-means cluster count k is selected using silhouette score, an unsupervised criterion — this does not optimize for alignment between clusters and clinical stages, so the selected k may partition the data by scanner site or motion artifacts rather than disease status.

Functional connectivity matrices and PCA decomposition are computed on the full dataset before splitting, encoding test participants' group-level connectivity statistics into the feature representations used during training — a direct leakage of disease-correlated information.

The scan-level random split does not enforce participant-level exclusivity; the same participant can appear in both training and test sets, allowing the model to classify test scans based on memorized participant-specific connectivity signatures.

The significance test specification ('paired t-test or McNemar') is inappropriate for a three-class macro F1 comparison and can produce anticonservative p-values, inflating apparent statistical significance.

The choice between logistic regression and XGBoost as the primary supervised model is left unresolved — if both are evaluated and the better one reported, this is an undisclosed model selection step that inflates apparent performance.
all_issues_adjudicated: k-means cluster count k is selected using silhouette score, an unsupervised criterion — this does not optimize for alignment between clusters and clinical stages, so the selected k may partition the data by scanner site or motion artifacts rather than disease status.

Functional connectivity matrices and PCA decomposition are computed on the full dataset before splitting, encoding test participants' group-level connectivity statistics into the feature representations used during training — a direct leakage of disease-correlated information.

The scan-level random split does not enforce participant-level exclusivity; the same participant can appear in both training and test sets, allowing the model to classify test scans based on memorized participant-specific connectivity signatures.

The significance test specification ('paired t-test or McNemar') is inappropriate for a three-class macro F1 comparison and can produce anticonservative p-values, inflating apparent statistical significance.

The choice between logistic regression and XGBoost as the primary supervised model is left unresolved — if both are evaluated and the better one reported, this is an undisclosed model selection step that inflates apparent performance.
